title: Premium, consistent HDR grade for a Caribbean vacation-rental villa listing set (interior + pool + sea/sky), delivered as web-ready JPEGs plus a client review board
grounding_note: Verbatim from the source brief: Saint-Martin (Caribbean) vacation-rental management company, ~100 villas/apartments/luxury rentals, Nodalview HDR source, "create a consistent, premium visual identity across our entire portfolio", "enhance and finalize the images while maintaining a natural and realistic look", and the explicit lists: Perspective/Geometry (vertical correction, straightening, lens correction), Exposure/Colors (fine exposure, white-balance, color consistency, natural contrast), Exterior (pool water, sea color, sky, vegetation enhancement), Interior (brightness/clarity, balance indoor/outdoor through windows, correct color casts), and the DO-NOT-WANT list (no fake-HDR look, no over-saturation, no unrealistic blue pools). Reasonably enriched for a runnable benchmark: I instantiate ONE representative property's three-shot set (a luxury interior with a sea-view window, a pool deck, and a wide sea/sky terrace) instead of all 100 villas, and pin concrete numeric targets (output 2048px long-edge sRGB JPEG at quality 90, a 16:9 hero crop). HONESTY/SCOPE: the brief also lists "vertical/perspective correction" and "occasional object removal of electrical cables, reflections, minor stains" — the Adobe connector has NO perspective-warp and NO AI object-removal/heal (A6 [X] list), and image_auto_straighten only levels a tilted horizon, it does not correct converging verticals. So the connector does the entire repeatable grade + the horizon-level + selective natural enhancement of pool/sea/sky/vegetation + indoor-window balance + the consistent export and review board; the converging-vertical lens-warp and any cable/stain cloning are explicitly left as the human's finishing pass (this is a "partial"-grade deliverable, stated as such, not silently dropped). No fake-HDR/over-saturation is enforced by using gentle vibrance + targeted single-color saturation rather than global saturation, matching the DO-NOT-WANT list.
one_line_ask: Take a property's three raw Nodalview-HDR exports (luxury interior, pool deck, sea/sky terrace) and finish them into a consistent, natural, premium listing set — level horizons, balance exposure/white-balance, gently enhance pool water, sea color, sky and vegetation, brighten interiors and balance the bright window view, then deliver matched 2048px web JPEGs plus a 16:9 hero and a client review board.
full_brief:
We manage ~100 luxury vacation rentals in Saint-Martin and need a consistent, premium visual identity across the whole portfolio. Photos come out of a Nodalview HDR workflow already roughly exposed; your job is to enhance and FINALIZE them while keeping a natural, realistic look — never a fake-HDR, over-saturated, or unrealistically-blue-pool result. For this batch, finish one representative villa's three hero frames: (1) a luxury living-room interior shot through to a bright sea-view window, (2) a pool deck with turquoise water, and (3) a wide terrace looking out to sea and sky.

Required, in order: straighten any tilted horizon; apply a clean auto-tone baseline; do fine exposure and white-balance correction so the three frames read as one coherent set (no green/magenta casts, neutral whites indoors); lift interior shadows for brightness and clarity WITHOUT crushing the bright window view (balance the indoor/outdoor exposure); then do RESTRAINED, targeted enhancement of the exterior color story — pool water and sea color enhanced via selective hue/saturation of the cyans/blues only (not a global saturation pump), sky improved gently, vegetation greens enriched naturally. Keep overall vibrance gentle. Export all three at a matched 2048px long edge, sRGB JPEG q90, plus one 16:9 hero crop of the best exterior for the listing header. Finally assemble the finished frames into a single client review board so the property manager can approve the look before we roll it across the portfolio.

Out of connector scope and to be flagged for the human finisher: converging-vertical / perspective warp and any cable/reflection/stain cloning — the automated pass does everything else.

inputs.name: interior_seaview.jpg
inputs.gen_prompt: Photoreal real-estate interior of a luxury Caribbean villa living room, shot on a wide lens at golden hour, HDR-bracketed look that is slightly flat and under-punchy straight from camera. Pale travertine floor, linen sofas, a large open window framing a bright over-exposed sea view so the interior reads a touch dark by comparison. Subtle warm tungsten color cast on the indoor walls, very slight green tint in the shadows. Natural documentary real-estate framing, realistic textures, real reflections on the floor, no people. Horizon through the window very slightly tilted by about 1.5 degrees. Photorealistic, not CGI-glossy.
inputs.realism_notes: Deliberately flat/slightly-dark interior with a blown bright window and a mixed warm/green cast so the auto-tone, exposure, white-balance, shadow-lift and indoor/outdoor-balance steps all have something real to correct. ~1.5° tilt gives auto_straighten a target. 3:2, ~4000px long edge.

inputs.name: pool_deck.jpg
inputs.gen_prompt: Photoreal real-estate photo of a luxury villa infinity pool deck in Saint-Martin, midday, HDR-bracketed but slightly desaturated and hazy straight from camera. Turquoise pool water that currently looks a bit dull and greenish rather than clean turquoise, white sun loungers, tropical palms with slightly washed-out green foliage, pale blue hazy sky. Natural real-estate composition, realistic water surface and ripples, real shadows, no people. Photorealistic, candid, not over-processed.
inputs.realism_notes: Dull/greenish water + washed greens + hazy sky give the selective cyan/blue saturation, vegetation green enrichment and gentle sky improvement steps genuine work, while staying realistic so the 'no unrealistic blue pool' constraint is meaningful. 3:2, ~4000px long edge.

inputs.name: terrace_seaview.jpg
inputs.gen_prompt: Photoreal wide real-estate shot from a luxury villa terrace looking out over the Caribbean sea and sky at late afternoon, HDR look, slightly flat contrast and a faint cool/flat color grade straight from camera. Stone terrace in the foreground, glass balustrade, the sea a slightly muted teal, a soft pale sky with light clouds, distant horizon line tilted by about 2 degrees. Realistic textures, natural light, no people, documentary real-estate framing. Photorealistic, not CGI.
inputs.realism_notes: Muted teal sea + flat sky + a ~2° tilted horizon exercise auto_straighten, contrast, selective sea/sky color and the 16:9 hero crop. Wide 3:2, ~5000px long edge so a 16:9 hero crop still has resolution.

outputs.name: interior_seaview_final.jpg
outputs.spec: Finished luxury interior, straightened + auto-tone + exposure/WB-corrected + shadow-lifted with the bright window view held, neutral whites, no green cast. sRGB JPEG, 2048px long edge, quality 90.

outputs.name: pool_deck_final.jpg
outputs.spec: Finished pool deck with naturally enhanced turquoise water (selective cyan/blue saturation, not global), enriched-but-natural vegetation greens, gently improved sky. sRGB JPEG, 2048px long edge, quality 90.

outputs.name: terrace_seaview_final.jpg
outputs.spec: Finished sea/sky terrace, leveled horizon, natural contrast, gently enhanced sea and sky color. sRGB JPEG, 2048px long edge, quality 90.

outputs.name: hero_16x9.jpg
outputs.spec: 16:9 listing-header hero cropped from the finished terrace frame. sRGB JPEG, 1920x1080.

outputs.name: review_board
outputs.spec: Firefly review board deep-link assembling the three finished frames + the hero so the property manager can approve the consistent look in one place.

connector_workflow.note: Begin upload of the client-supplied interior_seaview.jpg into CC storage so the connector can edit it.
Finalize the interior upload; returns the editable asset URI used by the photo tools.
Begin upload of the client-supplied pool_deck.jpg into CC storage.
Finalize the pool deck upload; returns its editable asset URI.
Begin upload of the client-supplied terrace_seaview.jpg into CC storage.
Finalize the terrace upload; returns its editable asset URI. All three client frames are now loaded and editable.
Level the ~1.5° tilt visible through the window. (Honest note: this only levels a tilted horizon — converging-vertical/perspective warp is NOT available and is left to the human.)
Clean exposure/contrast baseline on the straightened interior before manual fine-tuning.
Cool/neutralize the warm tungsten + green cast so indoor whites read neutral (white-balance correction).
Lift interior shadows for brightness/clarity (the indoor side of indoor/outdoor balance).
Pull down the blown bright window highlights to recover the sea view — completes the indoor/outdoor exposure balance without a fake-HDR look.
Export the finished interior at the matched delivery size: sRGB JPEG, 2048px long edge, q90.
Auto-tone the pool deck (pool_assetURI from steps 3-4) to de-haze and set a clean baseline.
Selectively raise saturation/luminance of the CYAN/BLUE channel only — enhances the turquoise water and sea WITHOUT a global saturation pump (satisfies 'no unrealistic blue pool').
Nudge the green hue/saturation to enrich the palm/vegetation foliage naturally; gently lift sky luminance.
Gentle global vibrance only (protects already-saturated tones) to keep the look premium-natural, not over-saturated.
Export the finished pool deck at the matched delivery size: sRGB JPEG, 2048px long edge, q90.
Level the ~2° tilted sea horizon on the terrace frame (terrace_assetURI from steps 5-6).
Add natural contrast to the flat HDR terrace frame.
Selective cyan/blue enhancement of the muted teal sea + sky, kept consistent with the pool frame's water treatment for a coherent set.
Export the finished terrace at the matched delivery size: sRGB JPEG, 2048px long edge, q90.
Second export of the finished terrace, cropped 16:9 to 1920x1080 for the listing-header hero.
Assemble the four finished, already-produced frames into a single Firefly review board deep-link for the property manager to approve before portfolio rollout.

chaining_note: Three client assets are loaded via explicit enumerated steps: interior (steps 1-2 → interior_assetURI), pool (steps 3-4 → pool_assetURI), terrace (steps 5-6 → terrace_assetURI). Each of the three editing lanes is a strict output-to-input chain: INTERIOR assetURI → straighten → auto_tone → color_temperature (WB) → dark_portions (shadow lift) → highlights (window recovery) → crop_and_resize → interior_seaview_final. POOL assetURI → auto_tone → single_color_saturation (cyan water) → hsl (greens/sky) → vibrance → crop_and_resize → pool_deck_final. TERRACE assetURI → auto_straighten → brightness_and_contrast → single_color_saturation (sea/sky) → crop_and_resize → terrace_seaview_final, with a second crop of the SAME terrace_sea output → hero_16x9. The final create_firefly_board consumes all four finished exports (interior_seaview_final, pool_deck_final, terrace_seaview_final, hero_16x9) — so the deliverable board literally emerges from the tail of every chain. Every step after its own two-step load consumes the immediately prior step's numbered output; no step consumes a raw asset URI that is not produced by an enumerated upload step.
difficulty_rationale: Long-horizon (19 connector calls, 13 distinct Adobe tools across the asset, photo-raster and board groups) with three parallel multi-step grade chains that must converge into one consistent look and one review board. Difficulty comes from ordering discipline (straighten→tone→WB→shadow/highlight balance must run in that order to be physically correct), from the restraint constraints (selective single-color saturation + gentle vibrance instead of a global pump to honor the explicit no-fake-HDR / no-over-saturation / no-unrealistic-blue-pool DO-NOT-WANT list), and from cross-frame consistency (the pool and terrace both use matched cyan/blue treatment so the set reads as one property). It is honestly a 'partial' grade: the connector does the entire repeatable HDR-finishing pass headless [C], but the brief's converging-vertical/perspective warp and occasional cable/stain cloning are out of connector scope and are explicitly handed to the human finisher rather than faked.

reverify.banned_capability_check: No banned capabilities anywhere. All 13 distinct tools are real Adobe connector actions: asset_initialize_file_upload/asset_finalize_file_upload (A4 asset load), image_auto_straighten/image_apply_auto_tone/image_adjust_color_temperature/image_adjust_dark_portions/image_adjust_highlights/image_adjust_single_color_saturation/image_adjust_hsl/image_adjust_vibrance_and_saturation/image_adjust_brightness_and_contrast (A1 photo/raster whole-image adjustments — no masks fed to lens-blur, none present), image_crop_and_resize (gen-free reframe/crop for both the matched 2048px exports and the 16:9→1920x1080 hero, which is a CROP of the wide 3:2 source, never larger than the source), and create_firefly_board (A4 board, consuming already-produced CC-asset exports). No generative expand/outpaint, no upscale/super-resolution, no text-to-image, no from-scratch art, no object/cable/stain removal — those (plus converging-vertical/perspective warp) are explicitly flagged in full_brief as the out-of-connector-scope human finishing pass, not faked. All steps exec_mode C (headless-confirmed).
reverify.notes: All issues in the findings were presentation/enumeration nits and are resolved: the pool and terrace uploads are now explicit enumerated steps (3-4 and 5-6), so steps 13 (pool_assetURI) and 18 (terrace_assetURI) consume asset URIs produced by numbered upload steps; the whole workflow is renumbered 1-23; tool_call_count corrected from 19 to 23 (accounting for the four previously-prose upload calls); distinct_adobe_tools unchanged at 13 (the four new steps reuse the already-counted initialize/finalize tools). No doability or IP/naming issues were flagged and none exist — the client is a generically-described Saint-Martin vacation-rental management company (no real named company/person), and Nodalview (source HDR workflow) and Saint-Martin (a real place) are not the invented client brand and require no rename.
